role_summary: NAACP General Counsel 1956-1968 (successor to Thurgood Marshall); argued for Oliver Brown in Brown v. Board of Education (1954); U.S. District Judge for the Southern District of New York (Nixon appointee, 1972-2012); argued 22 Supreme Court cases and won 21.
ai_reading: Carter appears as the top semantic neighbor of more entries in this catalog than any other figure: Julius W. Becton Jr. (entry 71) at 753, Matthew J. Perry Jr. (entry 87) at 378, Clarence B. Jones (entry 20) at 246, plus appearances in the top five of nearly every lawyer or institutional-leader page (McDew, Greenberg, Marshall, Williams, Finney, Hrabowski, Cox, Brown, Bailey, Cleveland Sellers, etc). The embedding therefore identifies him as the catalog's central NAACP-legal-network node, the vector around which the civil rights litigation cohort organizes itself. His own top neighbors are Matthew J. Perry Jr. (entry 87, count 311), Charles McLaurin (entry 17, the Mississippi SNCC organizer whose voter-registration work the LDF litigation supported), Harold K. Brown (entry 55), Oliver W. Hill Jr. (entry 94), and Cecil J. Williams (entry 14). The novel observation is that the embedding pulls his testimony as strongly toward the grassroots Mississippi organizer (McLaurin) as toward the South Carolina civil-rights-legal cohort (Perry, Williams), suggesting that even from the NAACP's national legal headquarters, Carter's recorded vocabulary reads as field-network-aware rather than institutionally insulated.
biographical_paragraph: Carter succeeded Thurgood Marshall as the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People's General Counsel in 1956 and held the post until 1968; during his NAACP tenure he argued twenty-two cases before the United States Supreme Court and won twenty-one [src: 2]. He represented Oliver Brown, lead plaintiff in the consolidated school-desegregation cases that the Court decided as Brown v. Board of Education in 1954, and advocated incorporating Kenneth and Mamie Clark's psychological research on segregation's developmental harm into the legal argument [src: 2]. President Richard Nixon nominated him to the United States District Court for the Southern District of New York on June 15, 1972; the Senate confirmed him on July 21, 1972 [src: 3]. He assumed senior status on December 31, 1986 and served on the federal bench until his death on January 3, 2012, at ninety-four [src: 3]. His memoir A Matter of Law: A Memoir of Struggle in the Cause of Equal Rights appeared in 2005 with a preface by John Hope Franklin [src: 2]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 108 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].
